Evidence: compare left-padding vs. right-padding with a masking layer; report whether sequence length distribution interacts with prediction error.

3. Claim: The threshold calibration strategy — setting decision thresholds such that predicted positive cases match historical readmission prevalence — is sound. Setting a threshold to match the training prevalence on the validation set effectively forces both models to predict at the empirical base rate. For the LSTM (which has continuous probability outputs), this is a post-hoc calibration step. For logistic regression with L2 regularization, output probabilities may already be reasonably calibrated. The design does not report whether this prevalence-matching threshold step changes AUROC or distorts the comparison. If the LSTM's probability outputs are poorly calibrated, forcing the threshold to match prevalence is masking a miscalibration. Evidence: report AUROC before and after threshold calibration for both models; report calibration curves to show whether the threshold-matching strategy corrects or masks miscalibration.

4. Claim: Nested cross-validation within the training set for logistic regression's regularization is adequate. The design uses nested CV within the training set only for the logistic regression baseline's hyperparameter selection, while the LSTM uses the held-out validation set. This is asymmetric: the LSTM gets access to the validation set's outcome distribution to set its hyperparameters, while the logistic regression does not. Under a temporal split, the validation set may have a different case mix than the training set; having access to it for hyperparameter selection gives the LSTM an informational advantage. Evidence: re-run the logistic regression hyperparameter selection using the held-out validation set (same as the LSTM's tuning procedure) and report whether regularization choice changes; if it does, the nested-CV approach was suboptimal for the baseline.
all_issues_raised: Bidirectional LSTM uses future visit context unavailable at prospective prediction time; gain over unidirectional LSTM represents illegitimate information access
Left-padding (vs. right-padding with masking) forces LSTM to process padding tokens before real data, biasing hidden state initialization
Prevalence-matching threshold calibration may mask probability miscalibration rather than correct it; interaction with AUROC reporting is unaddressed
Asymmetric hyperparameter tuning: logistic regression uses nested CV on training data while LSTM uses held-out validation set, giving LSTM informational advantage
all_issues_adjudicated: Bidirectional LSTM uses future visit context unavailable at prospective prediction time; gain over unidirectional LSTM represents illegitimate information access
Left-padding (vs. right-padding with masking) forces LSTM to process padding tokens before real data, biasing hidden state initialization

critic_raw:
This CNN sensitivity study for early-stage lung cancer detection has several important methodological gaps.

1. Claim: The radiologist consensus baseline is operationally meaningful and directly comparable to the CNN. The design sets the CNN's decision threshold on the validation set to 'match or exceed the radiologist consensus sensitivity.' This is a one-sided threshold search: it finds the lowest CNN score at which sensitivity equals the radiologist's. But sensitivity at matched threshold does not mean the models are operating at equivalent operating points — the CNN may need to accept much lower specificity to match radiologist sensitivity. If the CNN achieves radiologist-equivalent sensitivity at 30% specificity while radiologists achieve that sensitivity at 85% specificity, the CNN is clearly inferior despite matching sensitivity. Evidence: report both sensitivity and specificity for radiologists and CNN at the matched threshold; present the full ROC curve and annotate the radiologist operating point.

2. Claim: Stratified split by institution and disease prevalence ensures the test set reflects full dataset diversity. The split is stratified but appears to be a random stratified split, not a temporal split. CT scan technology, acquisition protocols, and reconstruction algorithms change over time. An archive spanning 5 years will contain images from different scanner generations. A random split will mix scanner generations across train and test, which means the model will see examples of new scanner outputs during training. In deployment, if the model is trained on historical data and applied to new scanner outputs, the training set will not include those outputs. A temporal split would more accurately reflect deployment. Evidence: report scanner model distribution and acquisition year distribution in training vs. test; test whether performance drops on images from the most recent scanner models vs. older scanners.

3. Claim: ResNet-50 with ImageNet pretraining, 3D volumetric CNN, or Vision Transformer are evaluated and the best is selected on the validation set. Evaluating multiple architectures on the validation set and selecting the best constitutes implicit architecture search that inflates the selected model's apparent validation performance. With three architectures, this is a mild form of selection bias, but it is selection bias nonetheless. The reported test-set performance will not fully account for the variance introduced by this selection. Evidence: report validation performance for all three architectures; quantify the gap between the best and second-best architecture on validation; estimate how much of the test-set performance is attributable to lucky architecture selection.

4. Claim: 24-month clinical follow-up is sufficient to confirm negative labels. Early-stage lung cancers (the target class) can be slow-growing. A 24-month follow-up window may misclassify slow-growing malignancies as negative if they are not yet clinically apparent at 24 months. The prevalence of early-stage cancer in the dataset will be underestimated, and some true positives will be incorrectly labeled negative. This label noise is non-random — it will be concentrated in the smallest, most slowly progressing lesions, which are precisely the cases the CNN is supposed to detect. Evidence: report the proportion of 'negative' cases with any nodule finding that was not confirmed malignant within 24 months; consider sensitivity analysis with 36-month follow-up if data allows.
all_issues_raised: CNN threshold matched to radiologist sensitivity without reporting paired specificity; sensitivity match at vastly different specificity is not an equivalent comparison
Random stratified split mixes scanner generations across train and test; temporal split would better reflect deployment on new scanner technology
Multi-architecture validation search introduces selection bias; test performance does not fully account for variance from architecture selection
24-month follow-up window is insufficient to rule out slow-growing early-stage malignancies; label noise is concentrated in the most clinically relevant lesions
all_issues_adjudicated: CNN threshold matched to radiologist sensitivity without reporting paired specificity; sensitivity match at vastly different specificity is not an equivalent comparison
Random stratified split mixes scanner generations across train and test; temporal split would better reflect deployment on new scanner technology
Multi-architecture validation search introduces selection bias; test performance does not fully account for variance from architecture selection
24-month follow-up window is insufficient to rule out slow-growing early-stage malignancies; label noise is concentrated in the most clinically relevant lesions

critic_raw:
This deep reinforcement learning design for adaptive traffic signal control has several foundational problems.

1. Claim: Training in a deterministic traffic simulator initialized with training-period statistics and then evaluating on real-world test data is a valid generalization test. The RL agent is trained entirely in simulation and then applied to real traffic. The simulation is 'deterministic' and 'initialized with statistical properties from the training data,' meaning it approximates real traffic but cannot replicate its stochastic structure (incidents, pedestrian behavior, unexpected vehicle arrivals). The agent will optimize for the simulated traffic model, not for real traffic. When evaluated on the real test period, any gap between simulator and reality (the sim-to-real gap) is untested and unquantified. This is the single largest validity threat: the experiment measures how well the agent performs in a simulation that was fit to training data, not whether RL-based control generalizes to real traffic. Evidence: compare the agent's wait times in simulation vs. reality on the same time periods; quantify sim-to-real transfer error; if sim-to-real gap exceeds the claimed performance improvement, the result is not interpretable.

2. Claim: The Double DQN agent is evaluated on the real-world test period as if it were deployed prospectively. Evaluating a fixed, pre-trained RL policy on a historical test period is not equivalent to deployment. In deployment, the policy would interact with the actual physical intersection and adapt (if online learning is included) to real feedback. The frozen policy evaluated on historical data is measured by simulating what the policy would have done given historical traffic inputs. But real traffic responds to signal timing — if the policy changes signal timing, it changes traffic behavior, which changes the next state. The counterfactual evaluation (what would traffic have been like under the RL policy?) is not what is being measured. What is being measured is the policy's output given historical traffic states that were generated under the fixed-time baseline. This is offline policy evaluation, and the design does not acknowledge the counterfactual evaluation gap or use off-policy evaluation methods. Evidence: use inverse propensity weighting or doubly robust estimation to account for the fact that test-period traffic states were generated under the baseline policy, not the RL policy.

3. Claim: Using block bootstrap with weekly blocks adequately accounts for temporal autocorrelation. Traffic patterns have multiple autocorrelation scales: within-day periodicity (morning/evening peaks), weekly periodicity (weekday vs. weekend), and longer seasonal trends. Weekly blocks capture the weekly periodicity, but if the test period spans 3 months (months 16-18), there are only approximately 12 weekly blocks. Bootstrap with 12 blocks produces extremely unstable confidence intervals. The design reports 95% confidence intervals, but with 12 blocks, the bootstrap distribution will be discrete and the intervals will be unreliable. Evidence: report the number of non-overlapping weekly blocks in the test period; compare block bootstrap CIs to stationary bootstrap CIs; if the CI width is comparable to the claimed effect size, the result is not statistically reliable.

4. Claim: Throughput as a secondary metric ensures that wait time reduction does not reflect reduced traffic volume. Measuring throughput (vehicles/hour) as a secondary metric is necessary but not sufficient. If the RL agent creates long red phases on minor approaches to give green priority to the main arterial, throughput on the main arterial increases while minor-approach throughput decreases. Macro-averaged throughput across all lanes could remain constant while the RL agent has effectively reallocated service from minor to major approaches — a fairness issue, not a performance improvement. The design reports 'wait time variance across lanes' as a fairness metric but does not explicitly test whether throughput improvements are distributed equitably across approach directions. Evidence: report per-approach throughput and wait time separately; test whether the RL agent's improvements are concentrated in dominant traffic approaches while degrading minor approaches.
all_issues_raised: Agent trained in deterministic simulator but evaluated on real traffic; sim-to-real transfer gap is unquantified and may exceed the claimed performance improvement
Offline policy evaluation on historical test data generated under the fixed-time baseline; counterfactual evaluation gap makes wait time comparisons non-causal
Block bootstrap with approximately 12 weekly blocks produces unstable confidence intervals; statistical reliability of reported CIs is questionable
Throughput as a secondary metric does not detect reallocation of service from minor to major approaches; per-approach fairness analysis is insufficient
all_issues_adjudicated: Agent trained in deterministic simulator but evaluated on real traffic; sim-to-real transfer gap is unquantified and may exceed the claimed performance improvement
Offline policy evaluation on historical test data generated under the fixed-time baseline; counterfactual evaluation gap makes wait time comparisons non-causal
Block bootstrap with approximately 12 weekly blocks produces unstable confidence intervals; statistical reliability of reported CIs is questionable
Throughput as a secondary metric does not detect reallocation of service from minor to major approaches; per-approach fairness analysis is insufficient
